coredata.dc:title: A natural choice towards water desalination: Improving solar-powered wood interfacial evaporation by lignin-based carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Renjie
Guo, Jun
Zhou, Pengfei
Chai, Yue
Xiao, Xiao
Xu, Jikun
coredata.dc:description: Solar-powered seawater desalination offers a promising solution to the urgent issue of water scarcity by utilizing the endless energy of sunlight, yet attaining high-performance wood-structured interfacial evaporation remains elusive. We report a facile approach that combines lignin-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and perforated bulk wood to improve the evaporation rate and photothermal conversion, wherein the as-prepared CQDs in the evaporator functions as a intensifier to light adsorption and solar-to-heat conversion. Owing to the photoelectric transition of lignin CQDs, adding lignin CQDs alters the natural structure and properties of wood interface with punched holes, thereby heightening the ratio of intermediate water in the device to lower the enthalpy of water evaporation with salt-tolerance and long-term serviceability. Fluidic simulation reveals that the punched large channels with high water flux provokes the salt concentration gradient in hierarchical pores. In the meantime, the concentrated water at the top layer transports downward by diffusion and convection, thus making the spontaneous horizontal and vertical salt exchange. Through the depositing CQDs onto the surface-carbonized wood, the optimum evaporation performance of 3.722 −4.003 kg m−2 h−1 with over 86 % solar-to-vapor efficiency under one sun irradiation is achieved in 0.05 wt% saline water. Owing to the 3D-interconnected porous channels and CQDs photothermal interfaces, the evaporator could operate stably in highly saline water and practical outdoor scene. This work sketch a blueprint of all-wood-based interfacial evaporators with excellent seawater desalination performance, pointing to a broader future for the coordination of sustainable biomass conversion and water purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Water desalination
Solar interfacial evaporator
Bulk wood
Lignin-based carbon quantum dots
Salt-rejection

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formation and properties of lignin-based carbon quantum dots
Water desalination performance of wood-structured interfacial evaporator

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of synthesizing wood-structured solar evaporator decorating with lignin CQDs. (b) Weight average molecular weight (M w), number average molecular weight (M n), and polydispersity index (PDI) of lignin. (c) Two-dimensional heteronuclear single quantum coherence nuclear magnetic resonance (2D HSQC NMR) spectra of lignin including side-chain and aromatic region (50 −140/2.5 −8.0 ppm). (d) Lignin substructures are identified in 2D HSQC NMR, including β-aryl ethers (A), Cα-oxidized β-O-4′ substructure (A′), resinol (B, formed by β-β',α-O-γ' and γ-O-α' bonds), phenylcoumaran (C, formed by β-5′ and α-O-4′ bonds), syringyl (S), guaiacyl (G), p-hydroxyphenyl (H), ferulate (FA) and free p-coumaric acid (pCA) substituents. (e) Total content of quantitative functional groups such as hydroxyl (−OH) and carboxyl (−COOH) in lignin. CS: condensed syringyl unit. CG: condensed guaiacyl unit. P-OH: the total content of phenolic hydroxyls. (f) Schematic of the formation mechanism of lignin-to-CQDs, including dehydration condensation, C−C crosslinking, π-π stacking, and carbonization nucleation.

(a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of MCQDs and ECQDs. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of (b) MCQDs and (c) ECQDs. (d) Zeta potentials of MCQDs and ECQDs. Fluorescence emission spectra of (e) MCQDs and (f) ECQDs. (g) Fourier infrared transform (FTIR) spectra of MCQDs and ECQDs. Schematic UV-Vis spectra (h) of MCQDs and ECQDs with (i) band gap energy (E g). Relative fluorescence emission intensities of MCQDs (j) and ECQDs (k) after adding various metal ions.

(a) Schematic illustration of light capture and refraction inside the evaporator during solar evaporation. (b) Schematic of lignin-based CQDs binding with cellulose inside the evaporator. (c) The potential paths of adhering CQDs to cellulose, including physical interception, electrostatic attraction, and hydrogen bonding. (d) Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of transverse and longitudinal pore distribution in wood-structured evaporator. (e) High angle annular dark field scanning (HAADF) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) images of CQDs inside the wood-structured evaporator. (f) Full survey X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) and (g) the high-resolution N1s profile of MCQDs.

(a) Compositional change of wood (cellulose, xylan and lignin) before and after delignification treatment. (b) Hydrophilic properties of surface carbonized delignified wood (SCDW) including the growth and transverse directions. (c) Hydrophilic modeling of wood surface. (d) Schematic diagram of liquid surface tension and capillary force inside the wood channels. (e) Comparison of photothermal conversion efficiency between Blank, DW, SCDW, ECQDs, and MCQDs evaporator with a NaCl concentration of 0.05 wt%. (f) Changes in the temperature distribution of the evaporator surface are observed at 30 s intervals recorded by an IR camera. (g) Mass changes of blank, DW, SCDW, ECQDs and MCQDs evaporator (0.05 wt% salinity) under one sun irradiation over eight hours. (h) Comparison of evaporation rates between blank, DW, SCDW, ECQDs and MCQDs devices in 0.05 wt% NaCl solution. (i) The evaporation rate of MCQDs-doped evaporator under different salinity conditions ranging from 0.05 −5 wt%. (j) The cycling performance of MCQDs-doped evaporator. (k) Illustration of the salt-rejection performance of the evaporator at 3.5 wt% salinity.

(a) Optical photographs of the surface of evaporator system placed in a container of 3.5 wt% NaCl and 1 g of additional solid NaCl crystal to observe the progress of salt rejection. (b) Schematic diagram of a solar evaporation experiment in a natural outdoor environment. (c) Processing images of preparing a CQDs-decorated wood solar evaporator with drilled hole-arrays. (d) Photographs of evaporation process under natural daylight to observe condensed water droplets. (e) Schematic structure and its simulation of the arrangement of small-sized channels and drilled large channels within a wood block.

(a) Fluidic calculation to simulate the NaCl concentration distribution inside the evaporator. (b) Simulation of the liquid vector distribution inside the evaporator during solar-powered evaporation process. (c) Changes in salt concentration on the evaporator surface during solar-powered evaporation process. (d) Schematic of the salt rejection mechanism of solar-powered interfacial evaporation. (e) Different kinds of water, including intermediate water (IW) and free water (FW), are present in the evaporator.

head.ce:title: A natural choice towards water desalination: Improving solar-powered wood interfacial evaporation by lignin-based carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Hubei Province for Coal Conversion and New Carbon Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Hubei Province for Coal Conversion and New Carbon Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Hubei Province for Coal Conversion and New Carbon Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430081, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biomass Science and Engineering, Sichuan University, Sichuan 610065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biomass Science and Engineering, Sichuan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Biomass Science and Engineering, Sichuan University, Sichuan 610065, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar-powered seawater desalination offers a promising solution to the urgent issue of water scarcity by utilizing the endless energy of sunlight, yet attaining high-performance wood-structured interfacial evaporation remains elusive. We report a facile approach that combines lignin-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and perforated bulk wood to improve the evaporation rate and photothermal conversion, wherein the as-prepared CQDs in the evaporator functions as a intensifier to light adsorption and solar-to-heat conversion. Owing to the photoelectric transition of lignin CQDs, adding lignin CQDs alters the natural structure and properties of wood interface with punched holes, thereby heightening the ratio of intermediate water in the device to lower the enthalpy of water evaporation with salt-tolerance and long-term serviceability. Fluidic simulation reveals that the punched large channels with high water flux provokes the salt concentration gradient in hierarchical pores. In the meantime, the concentrated water at the top layer transports downward by diffusion and convection, thus making the spontaneous horizontal and vertical salt exchange. Through the depositing CQDs onto the surface-carbonized wood, the optimum evaporation performance of 3.722 −4.003 kg m−2 h−1 with over 86 % solar-to-vapor efficiency under one sun irradiation is achieved in 0.05 wt% saline water. Owing to the 3D-interconnected porous channels and CQDs photothermal interfaces, the evaporator could operate stably in highly saline water and practical outdoor scene. This work sketch a blueprint of all-wood-based interfacial evaporators with excellent seawater desalination performance, pointing to a broader future for the coordination of sustainable biomass conversion and water purification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: A staggering situation indicates that the scarcity of freshwater resources is a critical global environment concern confronting society today due to the rapid population growth and climate change (Agarwal et al., 2018). Although water covers a large proportion of the earth's surface, ∼97 % of the planet’s water is saline or brackish that cannot be used directly for humankind, thereby underscoring the urgency of addressing this world challenge (Liu et al., 2023; Mekonnen and Hoekstra, 2016; Schewe et al., 2013). Harnessing solar power to advance desalination technologies for freshwater generation has emerged as a potent and widely-embraced solution. Taking advantage of the abundant and renewable energy of sunlight, the solar-driven interfacial evaporation delivers a sustainable strategy to enhance global water security (Chen et al., 2023; Fu et al., 2023; Kuang et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2023). To construct a solar evaporator with excellent photothermal conversion and rapid solar-to-vapor rate, it should possess superior light-capturing capabilities, micro- and/or nano-pore structures for the transportation of water vapor, and robust salt rejection properties.

At present, the usual studies have been focused on preparing various porous materials to meet the requirements of interfacial evaporator, such as nano-assembled aerogel (Gao et al., 2023; Li et al., 2020; Ren et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020), carbon-based materials (Gong et al., 2019; Xiao et al., 2021), nanofilms (Sirviö et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2020; Zuo et al., 2020), and in-situ doping with high-performance plasma for improving the photothermal conversion efficiencies (Shi et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2020). Of note, their relatively high cost, complex synthesis processes, undesirable environmental impacts, and poor salt-rejection capabilities could hamper the practical potentials of solar-powered water desalination technologies. Therefore, it is imperative to explore a high-performance solar evaporation system that is eco-friendly, easy to operate, and excellent salt-tolerance. As a natural choice, bulk wood biomass that presents the inherent advantages of hierarchically porous structure, hydrophilic property, and robust mechanical strength allows efficient water transport ability, highlighting its potential as a renewable and suitable material for solar-powered water purification technologies (Gao et al., 2022; Guan et al., 2020; Tu et al., 2020). We anticipate that the wood-structured seawater evaporation would ensure an accessible solution to the pressing need of producing clean water, aligning with the goal of sustainable energy-material utilization.

Aromatic lignin, an especial macromolecule of π−π conjugated structure with a high carbon content (≥ 60 %), holds the ability of absorbing light radiation and performing photothermal conversion to render it suitable for seawater desalination (Agarwal et al., 2018; Guan et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2023b). Note that, the paper industry generates millions of tons of waste lignin annually as a by-product from natural wood (Ding et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2024a). Up to now, most of lignin is always used as fuel for power plants or is casually discharged into rivers, thus causing the harmful influences on the environment. In this vein, pursuing efficient lignin utilization has become a research topic of great interest to address energy-water issues (Liu et al., 2024; Zheng et al., 2024). As an abundant carbon source, the conversion of lignin into luminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been developed in recent years (Chang et al., 2023; Hou et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2024, 2023; Zhu et al., 2021). CQDs have emerged as a compelling subclass of carbon-based nanomaterial due to the excellent biocompatibility, chemical stability, low toxicity, high electrical conductivity, and tunable optical properties (Wang et al., 2024b). These properties have brought CQDs to the forefront of many applications, including bio-imaging, fluorescent sensing, light-emitting diode and photothermal technologies (Batool et al., 2020; Sbacchi et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2020; Yao et al., 2022). Utilizing lignin as a precursor for the CQDs production to combine interfacial water evaporation endows it with the excellent light-trapping capabilities, due to the presence of various functional groups in lignin. In addition, the recycling of lignin into wood matrix to ameliorate the light-capturing and conversion is a natural choice for reducing wasteful resource.

To improve the interfacial evaporation performance of wood-structured evaporator, we report a facile method to decorate the surface-carbonized wood with array-arranged holes and lignin-based CQDs. We found that the CQDs-modified evaporators deliver excellent desalination capabilities and cycling stability, offering the superior evaporation rates of 3.722 −4.003 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun irradiation. By verifying the universal properties of fluorescence lignin CQDs, the direct comparison between the emission wavelengths of methanol- and ethanol-solvothermal engineered CQDs on the evaporation performance is demonstrated respectively. The as-deposited CQDs can alter the interfacial structure of wood to provide a wide light-capturing and excellent photothermal conversion. Fluidic simulation and calculation are established to reveal the water transport and salt rejection mechanisms inside the evaporator. We anticipate that the present work provide a natural guidance to improve the performance of solar-powered interfacial evaporator in the direction of sustainable seawater desalination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation and properties of lignin-based carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Native hierarchical wood channels with low-tortuosity create the prerequisite to expedite mass transfer via solar-motivated transpiration. The compatibility of wood with tunable reactive constituents provides its potential of CQDs decoration to alter the interfacial structure. Fig. 1a depicts the closed-loop schematic of preparing a sustainable solar evaporator including the combination of lignin-based CQDs and perforated wood. To validate the universal photothermal ability, we adopted solvothermal method to convert waste lignin into CQDs using methanol (MCQDs) and ethanol (ECQDs) respectively. Through electrostatic attraction, hydrogen bonding, and π-π stacking, the CQDs can be adhered to the cellulose chain and/or residual lignin. On the other side, the direct delignification and surface carbonization of natural bulk wood with artificial well-aligned channels was carried out to serve as the framework followed by infiltrating CQDs. It is generally believed that lignin structure influences its CQDs properties and photothermal performances. In Fig. 1b, the edges, defects, hybridized degree (sp 2/sp 3), and surface functional species of CQDs are dependent on the molecular weight of lignin (M w, 2750 g mol−1). A high polydispersity index (PDI) of ∼3.1 indicates the lignin heterogeneity, making it susceptible to form reactive phenolic aromatic hydrocarbons capable of preparing multicolor CQDs.

The two-dimensional heteronuclear single quantum coherence nuclear magnetic resonance (2D-HSQC NMR) spectra of lignin and its related substructures are shown in Fig. 1c-d. Through the examination of side-chain region (δ C/δ H 50 −90/2.5 −6.0) and aromatic region (δ C/δ H 100 −140/6 −8), a variety of lignin substructures that influence the reactivity of the macromolecule are discernible. Fig. 1c illustrates the representative cross-linked signals of methoxy groups (−OCH3), β-aryl ether (A, characterized by a β-O-4′ linkage featuring an unbound −OH group on the γ-carbon), Cα-oxidized β-O-4′ substructures (A′), resinol (B, consisting of β-β, α-O-γ, and γ-O-α linkages), and phenylcoumarans (C, formed through β-5′ and α-O-4′ bonds) (Xu et al., 2023). The basic phenylpropaoid unit of lignin, containing syringyl (S), guaiacyl (G), p-hydroxyphenyl (H), ferulate (FA), and free p-coumaric acid (pCA) substituents, are easily discovered. Their chemical formulas are described in Fig. 1d. Through quantitative 31P NMR techniques, Fig. 1e delineates the contents of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in lignin with a total phenolic hydroxyl (P-OH) as high as 2.4409 mmol g−1. The dominant species is G-OH of 1.2001 mmol g−1, followed by the S-OH (0.5598 mmol g−1) and H-OH (0.4837 mmol g−1). The condensed units of CG-OH and CS-OH are observed with the low concentrations of 0.0989 and 0.0984 mmol g−1, respectively (Zhou et al., 2024). Of note, the aliphatic OH with a content of 1.557 mmol g−1 is also presented to confirm the side-chain of aromatic lignin. The aliphatic OH hold the potential ability to exert oxidative process featuring the formation of carboxyls (−COOH, 1.5739 mmol g−1). The in-depth NMR provides a comprehensive view of intricate lignin architecture, highlighting the specific linkages and structural motifs to connect the reactivity and properties (Hou et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023a).

As shown in Fig. 1f, the possible mechanism of lignin-to-CQDs involves the dehydration condensation, C−C crosslinking, π-π stacking of polycyclic aromatic derivatives, and the deep carbonization to nucleation. It is widely recognized that β-O-4′ aryl ethers account for a large proportion of the overall lignin linkage, ranging from 50 % to 70 %. Adding nitric acid (HNO3) triggers a dehydration condensation process that disrupts the aryl ethers (e.g., the α-O-4′ and β-O-4′) to produce lignin fragments. In the meantime, the nitrogen can be introduced to dope the lignin fragments, thereby forming the functional groups such as –NH2. Owing to the cleavage of α-O-4′ and β-O-4′ aryl ethers, the proportion of phenolic S/G/H and their derivate are consequently increased during the lignin depolymerization. Through cross-linking and π-π bond stacking, lignin intermediates are formed between their derivate components to serve as the precursors for fabricating CQDs. Ultimately, the carbonization and nucleation are conducted to form CQDs that can aggregate various types of functional groups. During this process, the molecular weight, surface functionality, S/G/H ratio, and linkage composition of lignin are of importance to change the properties of CQDs. The aggregation-induced emission of lignin molecules binding with CQDs also influences the photoluminescence features and light-trapping abilities.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are adopted to unravel the unique lattice structure of lignin-derived CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2a, the lattice spacing of CQDs is around 0.24 nm, indicating that it has a graphitic carbon structure. As can be seen from the atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of Figs. 2b and 2c, the relatively uniform dispersion of MCQDs with a thin graphitic layer as compared to ECQDs, which is conducive to the light absorption and heat conduction. The enhanced dispersibility suggests that MCQDs could achieve a robust coordination while integrating with surface carbonized delignified wood (SCDW), which is pivotal for improving the efficiency of solar evaporation process. The surface charges of CQDs are characterized through zeta potential measurements (Fig. 2d). Notably, the surface charge of MCQDs (−57.26 mV) is higher than that of ECQDs (−10.96 mV), indicating that the surfaces of MCQDs possess a greater abundance of reactive functional groups (e.g., −NH2 and −COOH) as compared with ECQDs. A high quantity of surface charge underscores the enhanced affinity of MCQDs with delignified wood (DW). In Fig. 2e-f, the optimal emission peaks of MCQDs (510 −590 nm) and ECQDs (410 −500 nm) are dependent on the excitation wavelengths, presenting the orange and blue light-emitting under UV irradiation respectively. This difference stems from a higher solvent polarity of methanol than that of ethanol, thereby favoring the formation of CO, −OH, and −COOH groups in MCQDs to ignite a red-shift illuminant behavior. In contrast, the ethanol-mediated solvothermal processing is beneficial to promote π-electron related groups in blue-shift ECQDs (He et al., 2023). Moreover, the wide spectral absorption of 390 −500 nm of MCQDs indicate their excellent light-capturing ability as compared with ECQDs.

Multiple functional groups of CQDs are identified by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). Fig. 2g illustrates that both MCQDs and ECQDs display analogous absorption peaks at ∼3300 cm−1, 1700 cm−1, 1500 cm−1, 1200 cm−1, and 1000 cm−1, corresponding to the vibrations of the −OH, CO, CC, C−N, and C−H groups respectively. The presence of C−N peak signifies the successful doping of nitrogen into lignin-based CQDs. Importantly, the intensity of CO, CC and C−N in MCQDs is higher than that of GCQDs further discloses that the red-shift phenomenon of MCQDs. As depicted in Fig. 2h-I, the UV–vis absorption spectra reveals that the band-gap energy of MCQDs (4.79 eV) is slightly lower than that of ECQDs (4.85 eV). This subtle difference suggests a red-shift in the fluorescence emission of MCQDs, reflecting that their electronic structure affects optical properties.

To refine the binding capacities, CQDs are always used as the nanoprobe to detect metal-ions by utilizing their excellent photostability and solubility in solvents. We conduct an experiment that a fixed dosage of CQDs is applied to assess their selectivity for various metal-ions with a concentration of 1000 μM. As illustrated in Fig. 2j, the fluorescence intensities of MCQDs are quenched over ∼90 % by the addition of Fe3+ and Ag+ ions. By contrast, the as-prepared ECQDs display a significant affinity with Fe3+ ions (Fig. 2k). This selective response behavior highlights the tunable binding ability of CQDs as versatile platform for metal ion detection, wherein the MCQDs with abundant function groups (e.g., CO, CC and C−N) exhibiting a strengthening sensitivity as compared to ECQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water desalination performance of wood-structured interfacial evaporator
ce:section.ce:para: Besides the inherent response of CQDs to light, the hierarchically porous structure of wood-structured solar interfacial evaporator is of importance to control the water uptake, evaporation rate, and salt-rejection. The array of natural channels within the bulk wood allows the sunlight to interact in complex pathways, such as reflection and refraction inside the evaporator. Benefitting from the punched holes, surface carbonization, and CQDs decoration, the wood interfacial evaporator are assembled to floating on the water as shown in Fig. 3a. After loading CQDs onto the inner wall of channels, the photons can be captured and absorbed to induce the electron jumping and photothermal conversion. The CQDs reflect the part of light to realize the process of light-to-heat in the evaporator (Chao et al., 2021), relying on the excellent compatibility of CQDs with wood. After delignification, the anisotropic cellulose structure of natural wood are maintained to serve as the main skeleton (Fig. 3b). The synergistic interaction between lignin-based CQDs and cellulose backbone improves the conversion efficiency of light energy into heat, thus optimizing the overall performance of the solar evaporator. The potential modes of attaching CQDs onto cellulose chain are depicted in Fig. 3c. The CQDs can be physically intercepted within the interior pore structure of wood. Due to the large number of negatively charged functional groups on the surface of CQDs, electrostatic attraction can be generated on the cellulose surface. On the other side, the CQDs are strongly electronegative and interact with cellulose −OH and −COOH to form hydrogen bonding. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) is adopted to provide a clear visualization of hierarchical channels within wood-structured evaporator. Fig. 3d shows that the interior hierarchical structure of the wood is filled with well-aligned channels along the wood growth direction, wherein the capillary channels facilitates the seawater absorption during evaporation. The light-trapping properties of these capillaries are enhanced after decorating the wooden inner walls with CQDs. The maintenance of lamellar cell wall is conducive to ensure the integrity of the vapor flow in channels and rapid thermal conduction.

Employing high-angle annular dark-field scanning (HAADF) and energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping confirms the uniform element distribution of C, N, and O from the as-synthesized CQDs within the wood channels (Fig. 3e). The exact chemical condition of CQDs is examined through the X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS). Fig. 3f and S2a show the full XPS survey scan of MCQDs and ECQDs, including the specific signals for the elements of C, N, and O. As shown in Fig. S1a and S2b, the high-resolution C1s XPS spectra of MCQDs and ECQDs are decomposed into three peaks. The composition distribution of MCQDs is fitted into C−C (∼52.48 %), C−N (∼36.92 %), and C−O (∼10.6 %) respectively (Fig. S1a). In ECQDs (Fig. S2b), the compositions of C−C/CC, C−O/C−N and CO is calculated to be ∼51.10 %, 36.87 % and 12.02 % respectively. Three primary forms of oxygen species in MCQDs (Fig. S1b) and ECQDs (Fig. S2d) include C−OH (∼533.1 eV), C−O (∼531.5 eV), and CO (∼531.0 eV). The high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum of ECQDs shows the location of peaks at ∼400 and 402.8 eV corresponding to N − H and C−N configuration, respectively (Fig. S2c). By contrast, the MCQDs show an additional peak of N-O at about 401.7 eV (Fig. 3g), suggesting that the multiple nitrogen doping is formed in MCQDs (Yang et al., 2022).

After delignification, the structural integrity and well-aligned channels of wood are maintained to facilitate seawater transport. Fig. 4a summarized that the contents of lignin (∼7 wt%) and xylan (∼9 wt%) are significantly reduced after treatment with NaOH and Na2SO3, while the cellulose content exceeding 70 wt% (Lv et al., 2023). The removal of lignin and xylan is of significance not only to expose cellulose but also to oxidize the −OH into −COOH, which is essential to form hydrogen bonding for loading CQDs. The test of contact angle disclose that a super-hydrophilic porous interface is formed on the surface of evaporator after surface carbonization and CQDs infiltration (Fig. 4b). The hydrophilic interface is a critical factor to optimize the evaporation performance by rapid water uptake and successive transportation under solar irradiation. The presence of micro-pores is conducive to reduce droplet shrinkage as a result of surface tension, thereby effectively reducing the contact angle. Fig. 4c illustrates a microscopic modeling of the capillary action of absorbing seawater, wherein the water droplets in contact with the wood surface can easily penetrate the pores quickly, resulting in fast surface water absorption and good permeability. The as-prepared evaporator has hierarchical pores to increase the specific surface area and heat exchange (Fan et al., 2021), combining with the light-absorbing properties of the CQDs, which facilitates the utilization of thermal energy in the evaporator for the efficient conversion of seawater into steam. The surface hydrophilic properties can be attributed to the small pore size of wood vascular bundles (Fig. 4d). After being placed in seawater, water molecules quickly infiltrate the vascular bundle wall and then rise along the growth direction under the tension of the liquid surface. When the surface tension of fluidic seawater is greater than the gravity generated in the channels, the water column begins to transport.

The photothermal conversion efficiencies of evaporation experiments within a NaCl concentration of 0.05 wt% are summarized in Fig. 4e. After loading the CQDs into the evaporator, the photothermal conversion of ≥ 86% is achieved due to the excellent light-capturing ability of CQDs. It ultimately increases the photothermal conversion efficiency to 93.5 % since the wide light-emitting wavelengths of MCQDs as compared with ECQDs. The temperature distributions of different photothermal coating of MCQDs and ECQDs are depicted in Fig. 4f and S3, respectively. It is noteworthy that the surface temperature of the evaporator rises rapidly to 50 °C within 1 min. It takes about 5 min for the surface of the solar steam evaporator to rise to the maximum temperature of ∼60 °C (Fig. S4). By prolonging the time with a interval of 30 min, we recorded the mass loss throughout the evaporation process, revealing a marked increase in weight-loss during water evaporation with the integration of CQDs (Fig. 4g). The mass change of MCQDs (> 30 kg m−2) are higher than that of ECQDs (∼27 kg m−2), which is ascribed to the MCQDs have a broad fluorescent emission wavelength. It means that the MCQDs can capture more photos and then generate more electron transitions, thereby generating a higher amount of heat under sunlight irradiation. The evaporation rates can be easily compared in Fig. 4h. The as-fabricated evaporator leads to the superior evaporation rates of 3.722 and 4.003 kg m−2 h−1 using ECQDs and MCQDs-decorated SCDW in 0.05 wt% saline water, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 4i, the evaporator was operated in seawater with different salt concentrations ranging from 0.05 −5 wt% to assess the effect of salinity on the evaporation rate. The stable evaporation rate of ∼3.209 kg m−2 h−1 is achieved even at 3.5 wt% salinity. With a high concentration of 5 wt% NaCl, the evaporation rate of ∼2.799 kg m−2 h−1 is also observed, disclosing that the stability of evaporator can be affirmed with a concentrated seawater condition. The cycling performance of the evaporator was examined under a simulated seawater salinity condition of 3.5 wt% NaCl, suggesting the durable functionality even after three consecutive cycles (Fig. 4j). This stable performance indicates that the binding between CQDs and wood is robust within seawater environment. The evaporation structure was floated in a 3.5 wt% NaCl simulated seawater reservoir under one sun irradiation. Of note, no salt crystal or white solid particles was formed on the wood surface with artificial-drilled channel-arrays after continuously illuminating for 180 min (Fig. 4k), indicating the excellent salt-rejection performance of as-fabricated evaporation system. Benefiting from the channel-array, the increased salt concentration in the natural wood pores can be diluted by fast salt exchange between the micron-sized wood channels and the millimeter-scale punched holes via the pits on the plant cell walls, resulting in the nonclogging interior channels for water uptake and solar steam generation.

The salt rejection experiment was conducted to evaluate the self-desalting capability of the evaporator. As revealed in Fig. 5a, 1 g of sodium chloride crystals was placed on the surface of the evaporator. With the increase of evaporation time, the reduced deposition of NaCl salts is observed. It is witnessed that the NaCl crystals completely disappeared from the surface within 100 min, confirming that the NaCl can be efficiently exchanged via the hierarchical channels along the wood cell walls. The salt from the wood channels diffuses into the low concentration solution within the punched holes, allowing it to be easily dissolved back into the bulk solution for self-cleaning and stable properties. The water collection performance under natural sunlight irradiation was carried out in a glass dome. By placing the evaporator device in an ambient outdoor environment under the natural sunlight, water vapor was quickly generated. A thin condensed water film immediately formed on the inner wall of the top-layer glass (Fig. 5b), demonstrating the practical applicability of evaporator over a real condition. Fig. 5c depicts the morphologies of wood evaporator with drilled holes after delignification, surface carbonization, and CQDs loading. The evaporation rate under natural daylight at different times of the day is carefully measured and as illustrated in Fig. 5d which is dependent on the transitional solar intensity. An optimal performance with an evaporation rate of 1.624 kg m−2 h−1 is observed at thirteen o′clock. There is no apparent salts coverer on the evaporator surface, which ensure the wonderful solar thermal conversion as well as evaporation rate during practical test. To further validate the salt exchange behavior, a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) model was estimated for shedding light on the complexity of the liquid flow inside the evaporator during seawater evaporation. Fig. 5e shows the detailed distribution of drilled large-size holes and natural small-size channels within the wood block. Artificially perforated orifices with a diameter of ∼1 mm are well-aligned on the surface of the wood block, which serves as an essential pathway for the downward transportation of highly concentrated salt solutions. On the other side, the natural micro-channels with the diameters of about 20–50 μm are of importance to seawater uptake via capillary force and hydrophilic groups from the bulk solution.

The distribution of NaCl concentration within the drilled large-sized channels can be observed in Fig. 6a. The seawater is transported along the small-sized channels from the bottom to top surface, and the NaCl concentration gradually increases as the water evaporates from the surface of the evaporator. Due to the difference in osmotic pressure between large and small-sized channels, NaCl molecules begin to transport towards the large-sized channels and eventually diffuses into the bulk seawater solution. To prove the fluidic direction in salt exchange, the vector distribution of the liquid during evaporation are simulated (Fig. 6b). Under the same pressure gradient, we observe the water flux of within the drilled large-size holes is larger than within natural small-size wood channel. With a low velocity of flow in punched channels, the high concentration of seawater produces a reverse direction of fluid flow from the top to bottom. This alleviates the salt enrichment in the evaporator to ensure its salinity-tolerance performance. The change in NaCl concentration on the surface of the evaporator is illustrated in Fig. 6c. During the process of solar-to-steam, the vapor from seawater is moved on the evaporator surface, leading to an increase in surface NaCl concentration. In the meantime, the drilled large-size holes exert its salt-exchange function to block the excessive NaCl deposition and to reach a steady state eventually, further indicating the salt-rejecting capabilities of the evaporator. Fig. 6d simulates the salt-resistance performance of evaporator with the assistance of manually drilled hole-arrays. In specific, the water flux of drilled channel is higher than that of natural wood channel under the same pressure gradient. Hence the salt concentration of drilled channels is lower than that of wood channels, leading to the in-plane concentration gradients and synchronous salt exchange via the transverse pits of the lamellar cell walls. The rapid salt exchange then allows the increased salt concentration in the punched large-size holes to be transferred back to the bulk solution (Fig. 6d). Taking advantage of multi-directional mass transfer, the as-prepared evaporator delivers long-term stability and real-time antifouling performance during continuous solar desalination. The residual cellulose/lignin and loaded CQDs with the interfaces of wood evaporator hold the abilities to form stable hydrogen bonds with surrounding water molecules (Fig. 6e). At the same time, the intermolecular weak hydrogen bonds also can be produced between these water molecules. Differing in the features and strengths of these hydrogen bonds, water in the polymer network can be categorized into free-water (FW) and intermediate-water (IW) (Gu et al., 2023), in which the evaporation efficiency of the IW is approximately 86 times that of the FW (Hirata et al., 1999). In this context, the lignin-based CQDs catalyze the conversion of FW to IW via the hydrogen bonding interaction with FW. In short, the lignin-derived CQD plays a dual character of photothermal intensification and IW binder to synergistically optimize the thermodynamics and enthalpy of water evaporation.

Wood-structured interfacial evaporation are being developed as versatile solar-to-vapor platform owing to its tunability in hierarchical channels and ability to natural water uptake, yet the inferior photothermal conversion and salt-blocking properties are prone to the failure of stable and continuous steam generation. In the present work, a coupled structure design to improve the performance of solar-powered wood evaporators by drilling hole-arrays, surface carbonization, and infiltrating lignin-based CQDs. During the evaporation, the hydrophilic lignin-derived CQDs are conducive to photothermal intensification and IW conversion to ameliorate the enthalpy and thermodynamics of water. The rapid salt exchange is achieved by the drilled channels to improve the antifouling performance in solar water desalination. To prove the CQDs universality, we prepared two types of blue and orange light-emitting CQDs with tunable fluorescence intensity, delivering a high evaporation rate of 3.722 −4.003 kg m−2 h−1 (0.05 wt% salinity) under one sun illumination with a photothermal conversion efficiency of 86 −93.5 %. The evaporation rate of 3.209 kg m−2 h−1 at 3.5 wt% seawater concentration and 2.799 kg m−2 h−1 at 5 wt% sanility are also confirmed in bulk solution. By natural outdoor daylight illumination, a continuous desalination during seven-hour is obtained with a maximum evaporation rate of 1.624 kg m−2 h−1. The CFD simulation and fluidic models reveal the potential mechanisms of water transport and NaCl migration to explain the contributions of channel size, concentration gradient, and liquid flow direction into evaporation. The gradient salt distribution between the natural channels and drilled holes drives the transfer of NaCl crystals back to the bulk seawater, resulting in the excellent salt-rejection capacity of evaporator. We expect that this research guides the innovative design to develop wood-structured solar interfacial evaporator systems that have great practical potential in renewable energy applications, servicing to the biomass carbon-neutral, renewable energy, and blue water technologies.

Jikun Xu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiao Xiao: Resources, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Yue Chai: Writing – review & editing. Jun Guo: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data curation. Pengfei Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Renjie Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32171728 and 22008159), Wuhan Knowledge Innovation Project (2022020801020312).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou, P., Xu, J., Guo, J., Hou, X., Dai, L., Xiao, X., Huo, K., 2024. Driving multicolor lignin-based carbon quantum dots into selective metal-ion recognition and photocatalytic antibiotic decomposition. Green Chemistry 26, 6005-6018.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhou et al., 2023

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heteroatom-engineered multicolor lignin carbon dots enabling bimodal fluorescent off-on detection of metal-ions and glutathione

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou, P., Xu, J., Hou, X., Dai, L., Zhang, J., Xiao, X., Huo, K., 2023. Heteroatom-engineered multicolor lignin carbon dots enabling bimodal fluorescent off-on detection of metal-ions and glutathione. International Journal of Biological Macromolecules 253, 126714.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of tunable fluorescent carbon quantum dots from lignin and their application in anti-counterfeit printing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, L., Shen, D., Wang, Q., Luo, K.H., 2021. Green Synthesis of Tunable Fluorescent Carbon Quantum Dots from Lignin and Their Application in Anti-Counterfeit Printing. ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces 13, 56465-56475.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zuo et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifunctional nanocoated membranes for high-rate electrothermal desalination of hypersaline waters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zuo, K., Wang, W., Deshmukh, A., Jia, S., Guo, H., Xin, R., Elimelech, M., Ajayan, P.M., Lou, J., Li, Q., 2020. Multifunctional nanocoated membranes for high-rate electrothermal desalination of hypersaline waters. Nature Nanotechnology 15, 1025-1032.
